title: From Self To Others
study: A Study In The Life Of Moses
scriptureForWeek: EXODUS 2:15-25 When Pharaoh heard of this, he tried to kill Moses, but Moses fled from Pharaoh and went to live in Midian, where he sat down by a well. Now a priest of Midian had seven daughters, and they came to draw water and fill the troughs to water their father’s flock. Some shepherds came along and drove them away, but Moses got up and came to their rescue and watered their flock. When the girls returned to Reuel their father, he asked them, “Why have you returned so early today?” They answered, “An Egyptian rescued us from the shepherds. He even drew water for us and watered the flock.” “And where is he?” Reuel asked his daughters. “Why did you leave him? Invite him to have something to eat.” Moses agreed to stay with the man, who gave his daughter Zipporah to Moses in marriage. Zipporah gave birth to a son, and Moses named him Gershom, saying, “I have become a foreigner in a foreign land.” During that long period, the king of Egypt died. The Israelites groaned in their slavery and cried out, and their cry for help because of their slavery went up to God. God heard their groaning and he remembered his covenant with Abraham, with Isaac and with Jacob. So God looked on the Israelites and was concerned about them. Make sure you focus on the verses for each day, as these important events are taken from several readings of scripture across several chapters. These above are chosen to give an overview of Moses’ formation in Midian.
weekTheme: Boys flee, Men change Most of the world is focused on trying to get us to do the right thing. Our culture uses shame, guilt, peer pressure, laws and media to get us to do what it wants. It focuses on getting us to act the right way, not getting us to become the right kind of person. Before God really uses a man, he will form him into the right kind of man. He will humble him, change him, test him, get him ready, then call him. This week, we see the importance of becoming the right kind of man. One God can trust with his mission.

days.title: Failure Isn'T Fatal
days.scripture: EXODUS 2:15-22 15 And sure enough, Pharaoh heard what had happened, and he tried to kill Moses. But Moses fled from Pharaoh and went to live in the land of Midian. When Moses arrived in Midian, he sat down beside a well. 16 Now the priest of Midian had seven daughters who came as usual to draw water and fill the water troughs for their father’s flocks. 17 But some other shepherds came and chased them away. So Moses jumped up and rescued the girls from the shepherds. Then he drew water for their flocks. 18 When the girls returned to Reuel, their father, he asked, “Why are you back so soon today?” 19 “An Egyptian rescued us from the shepherds,” they answered. “And then he drew water for us and watered our flocks.” 20 “Then where is he?” their father asked. “Why did you leave him there? Invite him to come and eat with us.” 21 Moses accepted the invitation, and he settled there with him. In time, Reuel gave Moses his daughter Zipporah to be his wife. 22 Later she gave birth to a son, and Moses named him Gershom, for he explained, “I have been a foreigner in a foreign land.”
days.reflection: What do you tend to do when you screw up? Do you hide in shame? Do you beat yourself up? Do you wallow in self-pity? Do you try and forget about it and pretend it never happened? It’s hard to know how to respond when you mess up. It’s hard not to hide away and retreat. It’s hard not to want to quit. All of us are going to make mistakes along the way, and a key to growing is figuring out how to get past them. Messing up is inevitable; learning how to move on isn’t. You are going to mess up in your friendships. You may say stupid stuff when you’re angry or say stuff behind others’ backs to try and fit in. You are going to mess up with your integrity. You may look at stuff you are not supposed to or sneak stuff at friends’ houses thinking your parents won’t know. You are going to mess up telling the truth. You will lie, exaggerate, distort or leave out parts of things to get out of trouble. We all have. It’s a part of being a sinner and living in a fallen world. Moses faced a moment where he had to deal with his failure. He failed to deliver the people he had come to care about. He set about to help his people but ended up having to flee for his life. He wanted to be a deliverer, but ended up being a murderer. So Moses runs. He runs far away. Far from the people he wanted to free, far from the palace he was raised in, far from his past and his mistakes. He comes to Midian. Midian is in the middle of nowhere. Midian is nothing special. Moses thinks he can escape his past in Midian. Moses sits by a well. While at a well, a small opportunity arises. Local woman are being harassed by other shepherds when trying to water their flocks. Moses jumps up and rescues the women. Then he waters their sheep. The women say thanks and leave. This may seem like a small detail, but it’s huge. Even though he feels like he has failed as a deliverer, he still has the heart and instincts inside him. He isn’t rescuing a nation, but a few women. He isn’t solving slavery, but he is serving someone. He is doing what he can, where he can. The Priest of Midian sends his daughters back and invites Moses to stay with them. He accepts the offer, settles in and marries one of his daughters. He has children and slowly begins to rebuild his life. It may not be what he thought it would be, but it’s something. He is figuring out how to move on. It’s small, humble, faithful and even painful, but learning to live on the others side of failure is something we all have to do. How you learn to handle failure as you grow up will be so important. You can’t let it paralyze you. You need to acknowledge what happened, process the emotions, then move forward. You may have to do small things to get back on track, fight off proverbial shepherds, and water some sheep, but God will meet you. When you screw up, you may think that all is lost. You may feel like nothing can be done, and all is doomed. But it’s not, even if it feels that way. God is working to strengthen and form you; He will work to help you learn, get better, move on and become the kind of person he can use. But this time, you will rely on his power and process, not your own, and that will make all the difference.
questions: What is your normal response to things when you mess up? Do you hide, feel sorry for yourself, pretend it never happened, or just move on? Maybe you have some other kind of reaction? Can you give an example of this?
How do your friends tend to handle things when they mess up? Do you think these are good ways of handling mistakes?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share a time they really messed up. What happened, and how did they overcome it?

thoughts.verse: The godly may trip seven times, but they will get up again. But one disaster is enough to overthrow the wicked.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to thank God for his rich mercy. Thank him that even when you feel like giving up you can rest in the fact that he will never give up on you.

days.title: Encountering God
days.scripture: EXODUS 3:1-10 1 One day Moses was tending the flock of his father-in-law, Jethro, the priest of Midian. He led the flock far into the wilderness and came to Sinai, the mountain of God. 2 There the angel of the LORD appeared to him in a blazing fire from the middle of a bush. Moses stared in amazement. Though the bush was engulfed in flames, it didn’t burn up. 3 “This is amazing,” Moses said to himself. “Why isn’t that bush burning up? I must go see it.” 4 When the LORD saw Moses coming to take a closer look, God called to him from the middle of the bush, “Moses! Moses!” “Here I am!” Moses replied. 5 “Do not come any closer,” the LORD warned. “Take off your sandals, for you are standing on holy ground. 6 I am the God of your father— the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob.” When Moses heard this, he covered his face because he was afraid to look at God. 7 Then the LORD told him, “I have certainly seen the oppression of my people in Egypt. I have heard their cries of distress because of their harsh slave drivers. Yes, I am aware of their suffering. 8 So I have come down to rescue them from the power of the Egyptians and lead them out of Egypt into their own fertile and spacious land. It is a land flowing with milk and honey—the land where the Canaanites, Hittites, Amorites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites now live. 9 Look! The cry of the people of Israel has reached me, and I have seen how harshly the Egyptians abuse them. 10 Now go, for I am sending you to Pharaoh. You must lead my people Israel out of Egypt.”
days.reflection: One of the most important things you need to know as you grow up is this: Failure isn’t fatal. It may delay you, discourage you, haunt you, slow you, but it won’t kill you. God will never give up on you. God can redeem failure. There is no sin he can’t forgive. No brokenness he can’t heal. No relationship he can’t restore. No place he can’t reach you. No failure he can’t redeem. God will fulfill his purpose for you. Moses is proof of this. Moses must have thought his calling was done. It’s been 40 years, YES, 40 YEARS since he fled from Egypt. He has become a shepherd, a husband, a father. Egypt is behind him; he seems to have accepted his life. His first 40 years were in a palace, learning hieroglyphics, science, literature, and military tactics. His second 40 years were learning about sheep, pastures, deserts, kids and family. But then one day everything changes. God shows up and calls him again. God reveals himself in the middle of a bush that doesn’t burn up. Moses turns aside and God speaks to him. “Do not come any closer,” the LORD warned. “Take off your sandals, for you are standing on holy ground. I am the God of your father—the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob.” When Moses heard this, he covered his face because he was afraid to look at God. Several things happen here that are important to note: Moses pays attention. He is going to realize that God can meet him in the middle of his everyday life. Moses makes himself available to God. When the LORD saw Moses coming to take a closer look, God called to him from the middle of the bush, “Moses! Moses!” "Here I am!" Moses replied. It’s been 40 years, yet he still has a responsive heart after all this time. “Here I am,” he replies. God reveals himself as a God of grace. I am the God of your father—the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob. Abraham, Isaac and Jacob all followed God but made major mistakes. Abraham lied. Isaac lied. Jacob was a thief and stole his brothers blessing. God is a faithful God that works through broken people to fulfill his purposes. There is hope for Moses in this call. Moses has to separate himself and be holy. "Do not come any closer,” the LORD warned. “Take off your sandals, for you are standing on holy ground. Moses takes his shoes off because he is standing on holy ground. Holiness is about being separate, set apart. Moses is going to have to separate himself from his past. He is going to have to put his failure behind him. He is going to have to consecrate himself to this new assignment God is giving him. He is going to have to become God’s man again. Not a prince, not a shepherd. A servant of the most high God. He is going to have to learn to do things God’s way. Isn’t it amazing that God doesn’t give up on Moses, even though it’s been decades. You can draw on this for encouragement and hope. Even though you are young, you can know that God will never abandon you, cancel you, or forget you. Even though you may have moments of failure and seasons of sadness, God will keep working. He will work in you, and when you are ready, he will work through you, and you never know how amazing that will be. Moses has no idea all that will happen, but his willingness to say yes kicks off a whole new season in his life. When you fail, God will come find you. Make sure you say yes to his call when he does.
questions: The Bible says that God spoke to Moses when he paid attention and came close. Paying attention is hard because we live in a world with so many distractions. What are the biggest distraction for guys your age? How much do you struggle to pay attention?
If you could change one mistake you have made from your past, what would it be?
What does it mean to be holy and separate?

thoughts.verse: You have allowed me to suffer much hardship, but you will restore me to life again and lift me up from the depths of the earth. You will restore me to even greater honor and comfort me once again.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: If you have done something that is bothering your conscience, and you are struggling to feel God’s presence or worried he won’t forgive you, why don’t you confess it to him right now. You can do this quietly in your heart, or share it with your dad. Don’t let your sin silence God’s voice, He is slow to anger, abounding in love, and will call you into his purpose.

days.title: Compassion And Call
days.scripture: EXODUS 3:7-10 7 Then the LORD told him, “I have certainly seen the oppression of my people in Egypt. I have heard their cries of distress because of their harsh slave drivers. Yes, I am aware of their suffering. 8 So I have come down to rescue them from the power of the Egyptians and lead them out of Egypt into their own fertile and spacious land. It is a land flowing with milk and honey—the land where the Canaanites, Hittites, Amorites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites now live. 9 Look! The cry of the people of Israel has reached me, and I have seen how harshly the Egyptians abuse them. 10 Now go, for I am sending you to Pharaoh. You must lead my people Israel out of Egypt.”
days.reflection: If you are going to make a shift from living for yourself like a boy to caring about others like a man, you are going to need God’s help. What you do must be rooted in holy compassion, not human ambition. You have to know you are partnering with God, not just taking things into your own hands. This will require that you learn to trust that God cares about the things that you want to see changed. God’s message to Moses contains some powerful truths that let us know that he is a caring and involved God, not distant and unengaged. God says 4 specific things to Moses that reveals his heart. Then the LORD told him, “I have certainly seen the oppression of my people in Egypt. I have heard their cries of distress because of their harsh slave drivers. Yes, I am aware of their suffering. So I have come down to rescue them from the power of the Egyptians… I have certainly seen the oppression of my people… I have heard their cries of distress… I am aware of their suffering. I have come down to rescue them. God knows that his people are suffering. His eyes are open, his ears are listening, and he is going to act. His heart is to free them from oppression and bring them into a safe and prosperous place. God continues: So I have come down to rescue them from the power of the Egyptians and lead them out of Egypt into their own fertile and spacious land. It is a land flowing with milk and honey—the land where the Canaanites, Hittites, Amorites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites now live. Look! The cry of the people of Israel has reached me, and I have seen how harshly the Egyptians abuse them. God will rescue his people; he will act to set them free. But now comes the important part for Moses: he is that plan. God’s solution to the suffering of his people is the sending of his servant. Look at verse 10. “Now go, for I am sending you to Pharaoh. You must lead my people Israel out of Egypt.” YOU MUST LEAD MY PEOPLE OUT OF EGYPT. God is ready to work, and he is looking for a leader. Earlier in his life, Moses was moved to act on behalf of his people, but he took things into his own hands. He messed up and had to flee. But now Moses has been humbled. He is going to have to respond on God’s terms. Moses will become a deliverer after all, but he will be God’s man, not his own. This is a really important lesson to pay attention to as a young man. You have to learn to act in God’s timing. John Maxwell notes this about timing: The wrong action at the wrong time leads to disaster. The right action at the wrong time leads to resistance. The wrong action at the right time is a mistake. The right action at the right time leads to success. After what must have felt like an eternity, God is on the move again. He hears the cry of his people, and he has called his leader. Things are about to change for the children of Israel. God is on the move. As a young guy trying to figure out how to follow Jesus and love others in the modern world, you have to learn to listen to God in his timing, Don’t rush things, take them into your own hands, act rashly, or hustle. Wait on him; his timing is perfect. Move when he moves, and you will step into what he is doing in an amazing way.
questions: Do you think that God is intimately involved in our lives and really cares about us? Why or why not?

God’s plan to deliver his people is to send a man, Moses. Why do you think God chooses to use people rather than just acting himself? Do you think God would ever use you to help other people? In what way?

God says that he sees, hears, knows, and acts on the cries of his people. Is there an area of your life that you are struggling with that you would like for God to step in and act on? Why don’t you take a moment right now to lift that up to God.

thoughts.verse: So let’s not get tired of doing what is good. At just the right time we will reap a harvest of blessing if we don’t give up.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to thank God that he sees, hears and knows us and acts on our behalf. Thank him for being a God that loves and rescues his people.

days.title: Dealing With Doubt
days.scripture: EXODUS 3:11-22 - EXODUS 4:1-17 11 But Moses protested to God, “Who am I to appear before Pharaoh? Who am I to lead the people of Israel out of Egypt?” 12 God answered, “I will be with you. And this is your sign that I am the one who has sent you: When you have brought the people out of Egypt, you will worship God at this very mountain.” 13 But Moses protested, “If I go to the people of Israel and tell them, ‘The God of your ancestors has sent me to you,’ they will ask me, ‘What is his name?’ Then what should I tell them?” 14 God replied to Moses, “I AM WHO I AM. Say this to the people of Israel: I AM has sent me to you.” 15 God also said to Moses, “Say this to the people of Israel: Yahweh, the God of your ancestors—the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob—has sent me to you. This is my eternal name, my name to remember for all generations. 16 “Now go and call together all the elders of Israel. Tell them, ‘Yahweh, the God of your ancestors—the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob—has appeared to me. He told me, “I have been watching closely, and I see how the Egyptians are treating you. 17 I have promised to rescue you from your oppression in Egypt. I will lead you to a land flowing with milk and honey—the land where the Canaanites, Hittites, Amorites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites now live.”’ 18 “The elders of Israel will accept your message. Then you and the elders must go to the king of Egypt and tell him, ‘The LORD, the God of the Hebrews, has met with us. So please let us take a three-day journey into the wilderness to offer sacrifices to the LORD, our God.’ 19 “But I know that the king of Egypt will not let you go unless a mighty hand forces him. 20 So I will raise my hand and strike the Egyptians, performing all kinds of miracles among them. Then at last he will let you go. 21 And I will cause the Egyptians to look favorably on you. They will give you gifts when you go so you will not leave empty-handed. 22 Every Israelite woman will ask for articles of silver and gold and fine clothing from her Egyptian neighbors and from the foreign women in their houses. You will dress your sons and daughters with these, stripping the Egyptians of their wealth.” 4:1 But Moses protested again, “What if they won’t believe me or listen to me? What if they say, ‘The LORD never appeared to you’?” 2 Then the LORD asked him, “What is that in your hand?” “A shepherd’s staff,” Moses replied. 3 “Throw it down on the ground,” the LORD told him. So Moses threw down the staff, and it turned into a snake! Moses jumped back. 4 Then the LORD told him, “Reach out and grab its tail.” So Moses reached out and grabbed it, and it turned back into a shepherd’s staff in his hand. 5 “Perform this sign,” the LORD told him. “Then they will believe that the LORD, the God of their ancestors—the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob—really has appeared to you.” 6 Then the LORD said to Moses, “Now put your hand inside your cloak.” So Moses put his hand inside his cloak, and when he took it out again, his hand was white as snow with a severe skin disease. 7 “Now put your hand back into your cloak,” the LORD said. So Moses put his hand back in, and when he took it out again, it was as healthy as the rest of his body.8 The LORD said to Moses, “If they do not believe you and are not convinced by the first miraculous sign, they will be convinced by the second sign. 9 And if they don’t believe you or listen to you even after these two signs, then take some water from the Nile River and pour it out on the dry ground. When you do, the water from the Nile will turn to blood on the ground.” 10 But Moses pleaded with the LORD, “O Lord, I’m not very good with words. I never have been, and I’m not now, even though you have spoken to me. I get tongue-tied, and my words get tangled.” 11 Then the LORD asked Moses, “Who makes a person’s mouth? Who decides whether people speak or do not speak, hear or do not hear, see or do not see? Is it not I, the LORD? 12 Now go! I will be with you as you speak, and I will instruct you in what to say.” 13 But Moses again pleaded, “Lord, please! Send anyone else.” 14 Then the LORD became angry with Moses. “All right,” he said. “What about your brother, Aaron the Levite? I know he speaks well. And look! He is on his way to meet you now. He will be delighted to see you. 15 Talk to him, and put the words in his mouth. I will be with both of you as you speak, and I will instruct you both in what to do. 16 Aaron will be your spokesman to the people. He will be your mouthpiece, and you will stand in the place of God for him, telling him what to say. 17 And take your shepherd’s staff with you, and use it to perform the miraculous signs I have shown you.”
days.reflection: One of the lies you may be tempted to believe is that leaders never struggle with doubt. Sometimes we see people up front and think, “I could never be like that!” They seem to be filled with courage and confidence. But you would be shocked to know how many leaders struggle with doubt and insecurity. A. W. Tozer wrote, “A true and safe leader is likely one who has no desire to lead, but is forced into a position of leadership by the inward pressure of the Holy Spirit and by the press of the external situation.” Many of the leaders in the Bible struggled to believe that God could use them. Gideon was afraid. Jonah was a racist. Peter went back to fishing, Timothy was worried he was too young. And in this passage, Moses seems to have lost his confidence. The Prince of Egypt, one of the best educated men, one mighty in word and deed, struggles to believe God can use him. Look at how Moses shares his doubts. Lack of Credentials (3:11-12) 11 But Moses protested to God, “Who am I to appear before Pharaoh? Who am I to lead the people of Israel out of Egypt?” Moses has forgotten who he is. He doesn’t see himself as a man mighty in word, deed and wisdom. He sees himself as a nobody that no one will listen to. Lack of Clarity (3:13-22) 13 But Moses protested, “If I go to the people of Israel and tell them, ‘The God of your ancestors has sent me to you,’ they will ask me, ‘What is his name?’ Then what should I tell them?” Moses is now worried that he doesn’t know enough about God to be taken seriously. He doesn’t know God’s name; he doesn’t know enough about God’s ways; he doesn’t know enough theology to convince others to listen to him. Lack of Calling (4:1-9) 1 But Moses protested again, “What if they won’t believe me or listen to me? What if they say, ‘The LORD never appeared to you’?” Moses is worried people will think he is making his call up. What if they don’t take him seriously? Lack of Communication Skills (4:10-12) 10 But Moses pleaded with the LORD, “O Lord, I’m not very good with words. I never have been, and I’m not now, even though you have spoken to me. I get tongue-tied, and my words get tangled.” Moses thinks he lacks the proper public speaking skills. The one who was described as mighty in word now sees himself as someone who mumbles words. Lack of Commitment (4:13-17) 13 But Moses again pleaded, “Lord, please! Send anyone else.” 14 Then the LORD became angry with Moses. “All right,” he said. “What about your brother, Aaron the Levite? I know he speaks well. And look! He is on his way to meet you now. He will be delighted to see you. Moses may have lost his confidence, but God will not let him deny his call. To all his doubts and concerns, God promises to protect and provide for Moses. No Credentials? I will be with you. No Clarity? I will reveal myself to you. No Calling? My power will be with you. No Communication skills? I will instruct you. No Commitment? I’ll send Aaron with you. God will overlook Moses’ struggles and send him anyway. “Therefore, come now, and I will send you to Pharaoh, so that you may bring My people, the sons of Israel, out of Egypt” (Exodus 3:10). As a young guy, the thought of trying to step up to help others may cause fear, doubt or hesitation in you. You may even be making excuses as you are reading this thinking, “God could never use me.” But learn from Moses. God will give you what you need to help the people he loves. Just be humble, available, and teachable; that is enough.
questions: Do you ever struggle with doubts and insecurities about yourself? Would you be willing to share an area you don’t feel very confident in?
If you could change one thing about yourself to be more confident, what would it be?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share an area they have felt insecure in. Then have them share how God has helped them overcome this.

thoughts.verse: Don’t be afraid, for I am with you. Don’t be discouraged, for I am your God. I will strengthen you and help you. I will hold you up with my victorious right hand.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you from it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to speak to you and reassure you of his commitment and call. Ask him to ease your doubts or insecurities with his love.

days.title: Going With God
days.scripture: EXODUS 4:18-31 Then Moses went back to Jethro his father-in-law and said to him, “Let me return to my own people in Egypt to see if any of them are still alive.” Jethro said, “Go, and I wish you well.” 19 Now the LORD had said to Moses in Midian, “Go back to Egypt, for all those who wanted to kill you are dead.” 20 So Moses took his wife and sons, put them on a donkey and started back to Egypt. And he took the staff of God in his hand. 21 The LORD said to Moses, “When you return to Egypt, see that you perform before Pharaoh all the wonders I have given you the power to do. But I will harden his heart so that he will not let the people go. 22 Then say to Pharaoh, ‘This is what the LORD says: Israel is my firstborn son, 23 and I told you, “Let my son go, so he may worship me.” But you refused to let him go; so I will kill your firstborn son.’”… 27 The LORD said to Aaron, “Go into the wilderness to meet Moses.” So he met Moses at the mountain of God and kissed him. 28 Then Moses told Aaron everything the LORD had sent him to say, and also about all the signs he had commanded him to perform. 29 Moses and Aaron brought together all the elders of the Israelites, 30 and Aaron told them everything the LORD had said to Moses. He also performed the signs before the people, 31 and they believed. And when they heard that the LORD was concerned about them and had seen their misery, they bowed down and worshiped.
days.reflection: You have learned a lot this week. It may not feel like it, but you have pulled back the curtain and seen how the God of the universe works to prepare a man. You have seen how God has formed Moses into a man he can use. That doesn’t mean a man without fear or a man who doesn’t struggle with insecurities, but a man who is ready for what God has next. God has helped Moses deal with his failure. God has called Moses to his purpose. God has revealed Moses’ mission to him. God has confronted Moses’ doubts. And now that Moses is ready, God us sending him back to set his people free. It may seem like God takes a super long time and has a super slow process, but when he gets someone ready, a decade’s worth of stuff can happen in a day. When you are the right person, the impossible can happen easily. When you are not ready, it can take decades. Becoming a man is about preparation. In the same way you have to prepare for a test, prepare for a game, or prepare for an assignment, you have to prepare to become a man. Without this preparation, a guy will just drift. Drift from video game to snapchat, YouTube to TikTok, trivial hobby to more expensive trivial hobby. You have to prepare to break out of this cycle by listening to God and leaning into his process of formation. You are doing this now. You should have some godly pride in yourself. You are on the path. You are moving along. God is forming you too. It may not seem dramatic; it may be barely perceptible, but it’s happening. God is laying a foundation in you; one he can build on. God is planting seeds in you; ones that will bear massive fruit later on. And God is increasing your capacity so you can handle more impact and influence without collapsing under its weight. And then one day, you’ll be ready. And you will go, and it will be on. This is what happened for Moses. 40 years of formation in Egypt. 40 years of formation as a shepherd, and now 40 years of insane fruitfulness as a leader. And so he goes. Moses loads up his family, says goodbye to his Father-in-law, rallies the leaders of Israel, preforms the signs God shows him, and the stage is set. One of the greatest showdowns in all of human history is about to go down. But this time, Moses will be ready. He won’t run away in failure; he will rally and fight. Moses is a man who is ready to give his life for others. And he will have to, because Pharaoh isn’t going to give up his slaves without a fight.
questions: Are there any areas of your life where you can see yourself growing and maturing as a young man? What are they? How does that make you feel?
If you could fast forward growth in any area of your life, what would it be and why?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share an area of their life they can see God has formed and developed them in. How have they seen God use them in this area?

thoughts.verse: Therefore, my dear friends, as you have always obeyed—not only in my presence, but now much more in my absence—continue to work out your salvation with fear and trembling, for it is God who works in you to will and to act in order to fulfill his good purpose.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to thank God for his work in your life. Thank him for forming you into the man he wants you to be. Open you heart to anything he may want to say right now.

extras: WEEK 4 MANSCHOOL CHANGE A TIRE MANSCHOOL REMINDER The recipe for fun is pretty simple when you’re raising boys: add to any activity an element of danger, stir in a little exploration, add a dash of destruction, and you’ve got yourself a winner. - John Eldredge OPTION 1. ACTIVITY: CHANGE A TIRE Being a man is about preparation. It’s about learning to do things before you have to do them. This week, change the tire in your car together. (You may need to watch a YouTube video on your make or model or a do a run through first) Take the tire off. Put the spare on. Tighten. Take off. Put the tire back on. Let him pick some background music while you are doing this. Let him pick the food as a reward when you are done. Let him know that being able to do something before he needs to do it is a key to manhood. OPTION 2. WATCH Watch Cinderella Man Starring Russell Crowe. Fantastic true story about a Boxer who feels like his best days are behind him, only to find he has been formed for such a time as this. It has some boxing violence in it, but it’s a top film. It will stir something in your son. OPTION 3. DINNER AND DISCUSSION Take your son to your favorite restaurant and talk about formation and readiness. How he deals with failure. Where he feels like he lacks confidence and isn’t good at stuff. Where he feels stuck. Where he thinks there may be any point of emerging call.
FIELD NOTES PRAYER FOR MY SON
